prompt: Your team needs to classify thousands of incoming support tickets by category every hour, on a tight budget. Quality bar is moderate and latency matters. Which model choice makes the most sense?
choices: Always use the largest, most capable frontier model for every ticket
Start with a small, fast, cheaper model and only escalate hard cases to a larger one
Fine-tune a giant model from scratch before processing any tickets
Pick a model at random per ticket to spread the load
explanation: Exactly right. Matching a small fast model to a high-volume, moderate-difficulty task and escalating only the hard cases is the classic way to control cost and latency without sacrificing quality where it matters. Sharp cost instincts!

prompt: A developer notices their API bill is climbing fast even though request counts are flat. What is the most likely lever to investigate first?
choices: The color theme of the developer's IDE
The number of tokens per request — long prompts and large outputs drive cost
How many engineers are on the team
Whether the model runs on a GPU or CPU at the provider
explanation: Nice diagnosis. LLM cost is billed per token, so bloated prompts and verbose outputs quietly inflate the bill even at flat traffic — trimming context and capping output length is the fastest win. Great call!

prompt: You are building an assistant that must reason over a 200-page contract, but the document exceeds the model's context window. What should you design?
choices: Paste the whole document in and hope the model truncates the unimportant parts
Chunk the document and retrieve only the relevant sections per question (RAG)
Switch to a model with a smaller context window to force conciseness
Ask the user to summarize the contract themselves before each query
explanation: Spot on. When content exceeds the context window, chunking plus retrieval of only the relevant passages keeps you within limits and actually improves answer focus. Smart architecture thinking!

prompt: You want an LLM to generate unit tests for an existing module. Which prompting approach is most likely to produce useful, correct tests?
choices: Ask 'write some tests' with no other context
Provide the actual function code, expected behavior, and the testing framework you use
Tell it to invent functions that don't exist yet and test those
Ask only for the test names and fill in the bodies blindly
explanation: Exactly. Grounding the model in the real code, the expected behavior, and your framework is what produces tests that compile and actually assert the right things. Well reasoned!

prompt: Product managers want the AI to turn vague feature ideas into user stories. How should you prompt it for the best results?
choices: Give it the persona, the goal, and the acceptance-criteria format you expect, and ask for stories in that structure
Ask for a single paragraph and let the format be a surprise each time
Feed it only the feature title with no audience or outcome
Tell it to skip acceptance criteria to save tokens
explanation: Great call. Specifying the persona, goal, and an explicit acceptance-criteria template gives the model the structure it needs to produce consistent, ready-to-use stories. Nicely structured!

prompt: Your customer-facing chatbot occasionally produces toxic or off-topic responses. What is the right design response?
choices: Remove all logging so the bad outputs aren't recorded
Add input and output guardrails that filter, validate, and constrain responses before they reach users
Ask users politely not to trigger bad answers
Switch the model every week and hope the problem disappears
explanation: Exactly right. Guardrails that validate and constrain both inputs and outputs are the systematic way to keep a public assistant safe and on-topic, rather than relying on luck. Solid safety thinking!

prompt: An employee wants to paste a spreadsheet of customer names, emails, and account numbers into a public AI tool to summarize it. What should the team's policy guide them to do?
choices: Go ahead — public AI tools never retain anything
Redact or avoid sending PII, and use an approved tool with a data-handling agreement instead
Paste it but add 'please keep this private' at the top of the prompt
Email the spreadsheet to the AI vendor's support team first
explanation: Nice judgment. Sending PII to an unvetted public tool risks a data leak; redacting it and using an approved tool with proper data terms is the privacy-safe path. Great instinct for protecting customer data!

prompt: Teams across your company are quietly adopting random AI tools without approval ('shadow AI'). What is the most effective governance response?
choices: Ban all AI use and assume the ban will be followed
Provide sanctioned, well-supported AI tools plus clear usage guidelines so people don't need to go rogue
Ignore it since it isn't an official project
Publicly shame anyone caught using an unapproved tool
explanation: Exactly. Shadow AI thrives when there's no sanctioned option; offering approved tools with clear guidance channels the demand safely instead of driving it underground. Mature governance thinking!

prompt: You want an AI assistant that can look up live order status, check inventory, and issue refunds. What capability does this require?
choices: A bigger context window and nothing else
Tool use / function calling so the model can invoke your real APIs in a controlled way
Fine-tuning the model on last year's orders
A faster GPU at the provider
explanation: Spot on. Acting on live systems requires tool use / function calling so the model can invoke your APIs through a controlled interface, rather than guessing from stale training data. Great architectural read!

prompt: You give an autonomous agent the ability to run shell commands and delete files. What guardrail is most important before going live?
choices: Let it run fully unattended to maximize speed
Scope its permissions tightly and require confirmation for irreversible or destructive actions
Give it admin rights so it never hits a permission error
Disable all logging to keep the runs clean
explanation: Exactly right. Least-privilege scoping plus human confirmation on destructive, hard-to-reverse actions is what keeps an autonomous agent from causing real damage. Excellent safety-first design!

prompt: Your coding assistant keeps inventing internal API methods that don't exist in your codebase. What design change best fixes this?
choices: Tell users to just ignore the made-up methods
Ground the assistant in your actual repository via retrieval (RAG) so it answers from real code
Increase the temperature so it's more creative
Switch to a model with no knowledge cutoff
explanation: Exactly right. Grounding the assistant in your own repo with retrieval is what stops it from inventing APIs — it answers from code that actually exists. Sharp thinking!

prompt: Before shipping an AI feature that summarizes legal documents, how should you evaluate its quality?
choices: Ship it and wait for users to complain
Build an evaluation set of representative inputs with known good outputs and measure against it (evals)
Read one example output and call it done
Trust the model vendor's marketing benchmarks for your specific task
explanation: Great call. A task-specific eval set with representative inputs and expected outputs is the only reliable way to know your feature works on your data, not on a generic benchmark. Rigorous thinking!

prompt: An interactive AI feature feels sluggish, hurting user experience. Which approach best improves perceived latency without changing the answer?
choices: Stream the response token-by-token so users see output immediately, and cache common results
Make every prompt longer to give the model more to work with
Always call the largest model so it 'thinks harder'
Remove the loading indicator so users don't notice the wait
explanation: Nice. Streaming output as it's generated plus caching repeat results dramatically improves perceived speed without degrading the answer itself. Great UX-and-performance instinct!

prompt: Leadership asks whether to build a custom in-house model or buy access to a hosted AI API for a new feature. What should drive the decision?
choices: Always build in-house because it sounds more impressive
Weigh your differentiation, data, talent, and total cost — buy/use an API unless the capability is truly core and you can sustain it
Always buy because building is never worth it
Flip a coin to avoid analysis paralysis
explanation: Exactly. Build-vs-buy hinges on whether the capability is core to your differentiation and whether you have the data, talent, and budget to sustain it — most teams should buy unless it's truly strategic. Sound strategic judgment!

prompt: You are scaling an AI feature from a pilot to all users and need to justify continued investment. What should you put in place to track ROI?
choices: Measure cost per request against a concrete value metric (tickets deflected, hours saved) and monitor it as you scale
Assume ROI is positive because the demo impressed everyone
Track only the total spend with no value side of the equation
Wait until the annual review to look at any numbers
explanation: Exactly right. Tying cost per request to a concrete value metric and watching it as usage grows is how you prove ROI and catch unit economics that break at scale. Great business discipline!
